description: Replace fixed-duration sleeps in E2E tests with deterministic polling patterns to eliminate flakiness under heavy CI load.

userStories.title: Replace FSEvents fixed sleeps with retry-on-miss pattern in hot-reload-dynamic.e2e.ts
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want file-watcher E2E tests to use deterministic polling instead of fixed 500ms sleeps so that tests don't flake on loaded CI machines where macOS FSEvents takes longer to initialize.
acceptanceCriteria: All 7 occurrences of the FSEvents 500ms sleep pattern (await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 500)) after waitForLog(server, 'File watcher: Watching')) in hot-reload-dynamic.e2e.ts are replaced with a deterministic pattern

The replacement pattern writes the file, then polls for the expected watcher detection log within a reasonable timeout, retrying the file write if the watcher doesn't pick it up (handles FSEvents registration race)

The fixed 500ms sleep at line ~805 (before hot reload during in-flight dispatch) is replaced with polling the test server invocations endpoint to confirm the request arrived before triggering the hot reload
All existing test assertions remain unchanged — only the timing mechanism changes
userStories.notes:
File: e2e/hot-reload-dynamic.e2e.ts

There are two distinct patterns to fix:

**Pattern A — FSEvents readiness race (7 locations, lines ~309, 371, 424, 669, 881, 946, 1098):**
Current code:
```
await waitForLog(server, 'File watcher: Watching', 10_000);
await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 500));
fs.writeFileSync(iifeFile, modifiedContent, 'utf-8');
await waitForLog(server, 'IIFE updated for', 15_000);
```
Problem: On macOS, fs.watch() returns (and the server logs 'File watcher: Watching') before FSEvents is actually ready to deliver events. The 500ms sleep is a guess that may be insufficient under heavy CI load.

Recommended fix: Create a helper function (e.g., `writeAndWaitForWatcher`) that:
1. Writes the file
2. Polls for the expected watcher detection log (e.g., 'IIFE updated for' or 'Manifest updated') with a 3-5s inner timeout
3. If the inner timeout expires (watcher missed the change), re-writes the file with a trivial modification (e.g., appending a comment) and polls again
4. Outer timeout of 15-20s total

This handles the FSEvents race without arbitrary sleeps — if the watcher wasn't ready, the retry triggers a new FSEvents notification.

Note: Each of the 7 locations expects a different log message after the file write (some expect 'IIFE updated for', others expect 'Manifest updated', etc.). The helper should accept the expected log substring as a parameter.

**Pattern B — In-flight dispatch timing (line ~805):**
Current code:
```
const slowCallPromise = mcpClient.callTool('e2e-test_echo', { message: 'in-flight' });
await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 500));
mcpServer.triggerHotReload();
```
Problem: The 500ms sleep waits for the slow tool call to reach the test server before triggering hot reload. Under heavy load, the request may not have arrived yet.

Recommended fix: Poll the test server's invocations endpoint (testServer.invocations()) until an echo invocation with message 'in-flight' appears, THEN trigger the hot reload. Use waitFor() with a 10s timeout.

Note: The test already has test.slow() set and the server delay is 3s, so there's plenty of budget for polling.

userStories.title: Replace fixed 2s sleep with invocation polling in dispatch-resilience.e2e.ts tab-close test
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want the 'tab closing during in-flight dispatch' E2E test to use deterministic polling instead of a fixed 2s sleep so that the test reliably catches mid-flight tab closures regardless of system load.
acceptanceCriteria: The fixed 2s sleep at line ~674 (await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 2_000))) is replaced with polling the test server invocations endpoint to confirm the echo request arrived before closing the tab
The polling uses waitFor() with a reasonable timeout (e.g., 10s) and checks testServer.invocations() for a request matching the tool call
All existing test assertions remain unchanged — only the timing mechanism changes
